data.title: Amazon's Weak 2025 Sets Stage For 2026 - Buy
data.description:
Amazon's Lagging Performance Is Your Buying Opportunity For 2026

It's a common practice among institutional money managers to rotate capital throughout the year among the most liquid stocks - usually within the S&P 500 index - following their theses on relative valuation, end market shiftings, competitive advantages/disadvantages, etc. Over the past 5 years, we have seen such kinds of inflows and outflows, with previous laggards becoming performance leaders in a very short period of time.

[2024 performance]

So far this year, there's only one Magnificent-7 stock that delivered below 5% return, well below SPY's 15% gain - it's Amazon (AMZN):

To me, it's a great opportunity to buy this underperformance in AMZN because its fundamental profile has only gotten stronger, and the developments they've made on the AI front seem to be underestimated by the market, similar to what we used to see with Alphabet (GOOG) last few years. Next year, in 2026, I'm expecting to see AMZN as one of the strongest stocks among mega-cap representatives, hence my "Buy" rating confirmation today.

Why Do I Think So?

Unfortunately for my previous update on the stock, AMZN hasn't rebounded to the levels I was seeing for it. But the firm's finances haven't worsened. In fact, we saw another double-beating versus consensus in the third quarter, with revenues of $180.17 billion (+14.55% YoY) and adjusted diluted EPS of $1.95 (+36.36% YoY) exceeding the consensus views by 1.35% and >25%, respectively.

Their EBIT would have risen to $21 billion (vs. factual $17.4 billion in Q3) without FTC and severance charges because their AWS business kept gaining EBIT margin sequentially from 2Q 2025. AWS's backlog reached $200 billion at the quarter-end (October bookings alone exceeded all 3Q bookings combined, per the management's comments), with ARR hitting about $132 billion. The AI workloads are growing "three‑times faster than cloud at this stage of its development, according to Argus Research's coverage update on the stock (proprietary source), and I believe that AWS's full‑stack approach gives it a great competitive advantage over most other firms.

They've got the infrastructure (Trainium and Nvidia GPUs), platform services (Amazon Bedrock), and their own family of foundation models (Nova), all of which are constantly improved and optimised. For example, BofA analysts expect the see new updates at AWS's re:Invent forum in early December, especially regarding Trainium3, which is going to reportedly offer ~2x the compute of Trainium2 at ~50% better energy efficiency. For reference, Trainium is now a multi‑billion‑dollar business that went up by 150% QoQ in 3Q 2025, so Trainium3 is very likely to extend that growth momentum well into the next year.

Plus, at the upcoming event, we should see news on AWS's expanded Nova capabilities and deeper OpenAI integration after OpenAI's $38 billion multi‑year compute commitment to AWS was announced in early November. All this gives me some conviction that we're still in the early innings of Amazon's AI product cycle, and the upcoming event in a few days might make it more obvious to a broader group of tech investors.

Further speaking about Trainium3, I think the strategic point here is that Amazon is reducing its dependency on third‑party GPUs at the margin while still partnering deeply with Nvidia (NVDA). This should allow them to 1) control their own roadmap and unit economics and 2) offer differentiated price/performance to large AI customers. Unlike hyperscalers like Google and Meta (META), who diversify into TPUs and custom accelerators, Amazon's decided to build its own stack, which looks like a solid alternative choice. According to Cryptorank, AMZN is extensively testing NVDA chips' alternatives at its chip lab in Austin, Texas, so I'm really excited to see what AWS can roll out next week.

Rami Sinno, an executive, is the engineer in charge of chip design and testing. He is reported to have worked in the chip industry for decades. According to reports, he helped create the first two generations of Amazon AI semiconductors. Reports say that the Amazon chip lab in Austin, Texas, is packed with Amazon AI chips that compete with those of Nvidia.

The company is reported to have invented the cloud computing business fifteen years ago. Over time, Amazon started building the infrastructure that sustains it.

Another catalyst that I expect to lead to a narrative change around Amazon is the ever-expanding AWS Top Secret, Secret, and GovCloud regions. Seeking Alpha News recently reported that Amazon seeks to invest $50 billion into "AI and supercomputing capabilities for Amazon Web Services, or AWS, U.S. government customers", giving agencies direct access to Bedrock, SageMaker, Nova, Trainium, and Nvidia infrastructure behind the same security boundaries where Palantir (PLTR) currently operates. I think that these AWS sub-segments can potentially chip away at Palantir's addressable pie at the margin over the next few years, and even if it's more about redirecting new AI workloads than ripping out existing Palantir deployments, the opportunity for AMZN stock's repricing looks massive to me in case they manage to achieve some progress on that. For greenfield mission systems where the requirement is primarily scalable AI infrastructure, it becomes much easier for procurement teams to stay "inside the AWS stack" rather than layering a third‑party analytic platform on top, which seems true in domains that Julia Ostian (another Seeking Alpha analyst) alluded to when discussing Kiro and AI agents:

Companies won’t have other choice but to get AI assistants for coding, because they won’t be competitive otherwise. This is exactly what I predicted years ago with Palantir. I said that once companies adopt a productivity-enhancing technology, their competitors will have no choice but to adopt it as well. And this will happen with Kiro, or with Kiro alternatives. Companies cannot afford to be three to five times slower than their competitors. Large enterprises will accelerate adoption because they will see direct savings. Amazon already reported that migration tools built on these technologies saved customers 700,000 hours of manual effort, which is the equivalent of 335 developer years.

So, when people say we only see a supply but don’t see a demand for AI, I really don’t understand what more they would like to see as proof.

Another AI implementation is the AI-powered customer service agent that Amazon uses itself and saves a good money by doing so. Every AI agent reduces the workload on human reps, increases the speed of resolution, increases customer satisfaction, and restores enormous amounts of cost efficiency.

Source: From Julia Ostian' article on AMZN

I know that Palantir's core moat in the national security ecosystem is less about raw compute and more about ontology, and AWS Top Secret/Secret/GovCloud are primarily infrastructure and platform layers. But the line between "infrastructure" and "application" seems to be getting blurrier every quarter as Amazon pushes more data services, AI agents, and domain‑specific toolkits into these secure regions, expanding its TAM to the areas it didn't serve before. It's not a direct AMZN/PLTR comparison, though. I'm just assuming that AMZN can get at least some part of PLTR's valuation premium in the next few years.

To date, AMZN doesn't look cheap at all. It's trading at 32.5 forward P/E, which is significantly lower when we zoom out and see the past historical figures, but it's still a lot to me on an absolute basis.

And AMZN's free cash flows are falling sharply because of the rising CAPEX needs, so it presses the valuation and makes the current multiples quite fragile, prospectively.

But with the change in AMZN's narrative that I assumed above for 2026, I don't think that the stock should see its P/E ratio go too far below 30x (on a forward basis). Also, we should remember that AMZN's ROIC has climbed to ~16.5%, up from ~3% in 2016, so this fact justifies some sort of stability to the current P/E multiple in the next couple of years.

Anyway, at 30x, and assuming that the beating track record continues further, and AMZN records a 15% premium on FY2027 EPS, the resulting price target would amount to ~$270.48 per share. This is a 12-month upside potential of over 18%, which can be even bigger if AMZN gets to a higher P/E next year.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

My thesis is based on specific catalysts that may not play out the way I expect them to. For example, in case Amazon's AI projects move more slowly to production/monetisation, or if its large customers increasingly standardise on rival stacks like Azure/OpenAI, or Google/TPU, AWS's AI workloads will no longer continue to grow at the past elevated pace. Plus, the heavy CAPEX they've spent on AWS Top Secret/Secret/GovCloud might run into slow procurement cycles and ultimately lead to very low ROIC.

Also, there's a core assumption from my end that next year, there should be a capital rotation to AMZN. But in reality, if markets decide to massively rotate out of "AI winners" on bubble fears, Amazon's FWD P/E ratio could easily de‑rate below my assumed ~30x, especially if any of the other issues/risks materialise at the same time.

The Bottom Line

I know that the risks of buying AMZN for >30x forward earnings seem to be high, but the company's business growth and existing moat are enough to justify this premium, in my opinion. In fact, I believe the premium can rise higher than that in the future if the company finds new TAM pockets (in the government area, for example), or if they can demonstrate that Trainium3 can meaningfully shift AI demand onto its own silicon stack. I see an upside of over 18% in the next 12 months, but Wall Street is even more bullish, seeing over 28% in AMZN's growth potential at the midpoint.

Based on all the above, I decided to keep my "Buy" rating on AMZN unchanged for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: FBY: Turning Meta's Pullback Into Weekly Income
data.description:
Overview

After assessing many YieldMax funds over the last year, I've been able to measure the performance, use cases, and payout sustainability through different market environments. With that said, I believe that these high-yield funds are best utilized as an accompanying position for investors that already have an established position in the underlying equity a fund tracks. YieldMax META Option Income Strategy ETF (FBY) aims to generate large double-digit distributions from its option strategy, while indirectly mimicking the daily price movement of Meta Platforms (META).

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FBY's share price has fallen by 30.86%. Unfortunately, these declines are typical across the YieldMax suite. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 1.87% over the same time frame. While this may seem like lackluster performance, I believe that a fund like FBY has a very specific use case. FBY now has a starting dividend yield around 37.7% and issues payouts on a weekly basis, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high-income generation.

When I previously covered META, I issued a hold rating due to the fact that META traded near all-time highs at the time. I felt like it didn't make sense to initiate a position in such a high-risk fund since I suspected a pullback at some point. Now that META has retreated from its all-time highs, FBY's share price has fallen significantly in the last few months. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's potential and utility going forward.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, FBY has total net assets of $146.3M and has a gross expense ratio of 0.99%. FBY is actively managed as the fund seeks to generate weekly income from selling call options on META. However, the caveat is that FBY implements a synthetic option strategy, which simply means that FBY doesn't actually own any common shares of META. Therefore, the fund is writing options against an asset that it doesn't actually own, which can increase the overall risk profile and price deterioration.

As we can see from the holdings below, FBY is primarily backed by U.S. Treasury bills. Additionally, FBY has an active call option that expires on November 28th, while another call option for $650 per share expires in January of 2026. Since FBY implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) option writing strategy, this helps the fund capture some upside growth through market rallies. However, the growth is ultimately limited to whatever the selected strike price is.

I currently own twelve different YieldMax funds, and the reality is that these large distributions are simply unsustainable. Funds like FBY are essentially self-liquidating, but it's by design. The only scenario where FBY's share price will see its share price increase over time is if META experiences a strong rally that happens rapidly. This can be driven by a strong earnings report, new product developments within the AI space, or even just the overall momentum of the technology sector. Despite this weakness, FBY is still a solid choice for investors that are looking to capitalize on the uncertainty and volatility of META's stock price.

META recently reported its Q3 earnings, and the results were strong. For instance, total revenue for the quarter amounted to $51.24B, which represented a 26.2% year-over-year increase. However, EPS landed at $1.05 per share, which caused META to miss estimates by $5.66 per share. The caveat is that EPS was impacted by a one-time tax-related charge, so the strength of earnings seemed to be a bit misinterpreted. Additionally, META pulled back from its highs due to concerns over the company's large capex spending. For reference, META confirmed $600B in capex spending to build out AI infrastructure in the US.

As we can see below, META's share price has declined by more than 15.7% over the last three months. As a result, FBY's share price has declined by more than 20.6% over the same period, and I believe this is the perfect opportunity to add shares.

Performance Insights

Since the option strategy limits the growth potential for the fund, FBY is likely to underperform META over time. Therefore, investors need to understand that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a high total return over time. Just to demonstrate this, we can measure FBY's performance since its inception. FBY's share price has declined by approximately 37.75% since inception. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 61.75% over the same time frame.

After observing the performance of these single-stock YieldMax funds, I believe that a position in FBY makes the most sense as an accompanying position. For instance, META sits as one of my top holdings, and I plan to maintain a long-term position as I am bullish on the company's future growth and its ability to participate in the growing demand across the AI sector. However, I believe that the way upward will be filled with volatility and uncertainty, which is the environment for FBY. I believe the fund's performance outlook can be summarized in three scenarios:

  1. META's Stock Price Rises = FBY is likely to see minimal price increases, and the distributions may increase as the option premiums are larger.
  2. META's Stock Price Is Flat/Choppy = FBY's share price may trade within a consistent range as long as META doesn't experience any rapid declines. The distributions may also remain within a consistent range, which can lead to outperformance.
  3. META's Stock Price Declines = Depending on the severity of the pullback, there's a good chance that FBY will erode investor capital and distributions may get smaller over time.

With these concepts in mind, I believe that FBY makes the most sense if you are bullish on the underlying movement of META in the near term. When measuring FBY's performance since inception, it's clear that the META's outperformance gap will continue to widen over time, assuming that earnings continue to grow. This is why it makes the most sense to utilize FBY as an accompanying position.

In the future, I plan to revisit FBY to build out a more direct comparison to Roundhill META WeeklyPay ETF (METW). The fund has an inception dating back to June of 2025, so the operating history is very short, and it wouldn't make sense to complete an in-depth comparison yet. METW offers a ~41% dividend yield and also pays out on a weekly basis. The main differentiator is that METW doesn't utilize options. Instead, METW uses leverage to gain 1.2x exposure to the underlying shares of META. When running a quick performance comparison, we can see that FBY has outperformed so far.

Dividend Risk & Utility

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.0904 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 37.7%. Despite the high yield, investors should be aware that new payouts are declared every week, so there's a chance that the estimated annual yield will be different by the time you read this. Since payouts are determined based on the success of the written options, momentum of META, and the implied volatility levels, the distribution amounts will vary every week. Therefore, FBY is not the best choice for investors seeking a consistent payout range.

Additionally, the dividend history may look skewed because the fund previously paid out distributions on a monthly basis. The weekly frequency is a new system that was implemented in October of 2025. This change was made simply to cater to the growing demand for weekly income generation amongst investors. My main concern is that FBY's payouts will slowly deteriorate over time. Since the fund's large yield typically translates to a declining NAV, the payouts are typically adjusted as well. Therefore, FBY may not be the simplest buy-and-hold fund and may require investors to reinvest portions of their distributions to keep payouts similar over time.

However, we are talking about a dividend yield above 37%. This means there are some creative uses for the income received. Personally, I hope to maintain a position for long enough until I reach house money status, which is the cross point where an investor receives their initial investment back in distributions. For instance, a $10,000 investment is currently estimated to produce ~$3,778 in annual income and will help investors reach house money status in less than three years. This is a simplified approach to the concept, which assumes that payouts will remain within a consistent range.

$10,000 investment / $3,778 annual income = 2.64 years.

At the time of my last coverage, I stated that the distributions are typically tax-efficient due to their reliance on return of capital. However, it's important to note that these payout methods can change over time. For instance, the most recent payout was only made up of 25.85% of return of capital, which indicates that the remaining portion of the payouts was likely fueled by net investment income.

So if you want to limit the potential tax liability from FBY, a position would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account. Maintaining exposure to FBY within a regular taxable account can lead to higher than anticipated tax consequences for investors. When it comes to the utility of distributions, I believe that the weekly frequency allows us to implement a dividend wheel method. For instance, I currently reinvest a portion of my weekly distributions into unrelated growth positions that are attractively valued. This helps offset any losses from these higher-yield income funds.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on FBY to a buy due to META's recent pullback. I believe that FBY is an efficient way to capitalize on the volatility and uncertainty of the price movements in the near term. However, FBY's price is still likely to decline and underperform META over the long term. Therefore, I believe that the fund is best utilized as an accompanying position for investors that already have an existing position in META. If you are bullish on META, FBY is a great way to turn the market's uncertainty into weekly income.

data.title: Autodesk Gave Up Most After-Hours Gains To Close At Fair Valuation
data.description:
Autodesk (ADSK) surged 8% during after-hours trading following the release of FQ3 earnings yet quickly erased most of those profits to end the trading day with only 2% in the green. That was reason enough to have a deeper look at where the stock stands fundamentally and in the bigger valuation picture.

While this is my first time covering Autodesk on Seeking Alpha, I had already analyzed the software company as part of a German research series back in November 2023. At that time, we considered the stock worth buying out of a stage of undervaluation. This has resulted in a solid 49% gain to date, almost in line with the S&P 500. Let us now analyze whether this has now closed the stock’s prior undervaluation-gap.

GAAP Or Non-GAAP Valuation?

It is noteworthy that Autodesk’s GAAP and non-GAAP earnings and margins differ significantly from one another. The company’s Q3 operating margin, for example, was 38% on a non-GAAP basis and only 25% based on GAAP. The GAAP to non-GAAP EPS difference is similarly large. The most significant contributing factor to this difference is stock-based compensation, as highlighted below. Therefore, investors must first decide for themselves which of those earnings figures they want to build upon.

Looking at the company’s trading multiples, it appears that investors clearly focus on non-GAAP metrics. Otherwise, a GAAP P/E of around 60x would appear highly unjustifiable. Non-GAAP (adjusted) P/E, on the other hand, is only around 30x.

Under the assumption that Autodesk’s adjusted P/E ratio is the metric that best reflects the company’s valuation, the stock’s 10-year valuation average of 45x would at first glance suggest that the company is still cheap. However, we must acknowledge that this long-term average would include both times with negative EPS and periods of irrationally high valuations until 2021.

Therefore, it appears more reasonable to scale down the valuation average to a 3-year timeframe, during which the company’s adjusted P/E averaged 30x. Based on this approach, Autodesk would now be trading exactly at its fair value. This, along with some arguable ambiguity concerning stock-based compensation, prompts me to assign a rather neutral Hold rating to Autodesk at this point. However, a $390 price target for FY 2028 based on consensus estimates and a 30x assumption for adj. P/E would still imply adequate double-digit returns with Autodesk.

When looking at free cash flow relative to adjusted earnings, it becomes evident that over the long run, adjusted earnings are a good reflection of the company’s free cash flow, as stock-based compensation expenses do not lead to cash outflows.

Within shorter time frames, however, free cash flow was more volatile than adj. earnings, which was partly attributable to the company’s past practice of billing lump sums to their customers, which were not necessarily in-line with Autodesk’s revenue recognition.

Now the company has switched its billing practices, which should not only lead to more in-line earnings and cash flow developments going forward but also distort some reported figures during the transition phase. Therefore, we should mainly focus on Autodesk’s figures adjusted for these effects.

Focus On Adjusted-For-Transactional Changes

As for FY 2026, which will end in January 2026, Autodesk expects to have generated roughly $2.3 billion in free cash flow, implying a significant increase of 45% YoY, mainly due to the aforementioned one-time catch-up effect of faster revenue-to-cash conversion amid the transition of the billing logic.

Billings themselves are expected to reach $7.5 billion, up 25%, but only 20% adjusted for the new model, and revenues will be almost $7.2 billion, up 17% as reported but only 11% adjusted for the new model. What is now a transitional tailwind, will expectedly turn into a headwind in the next fiscal year, as the effects of the billing transition will no longer exist.

With that, we should continue to build upon management’s adjusted guidance in the 11% range for revenue growth. This is roughly in line with FY 2024 and FY 2025 YoY topline growth and represents a continued slowed yet solid double-digit pace compared to growth rates up to the high twenties in the years prior.

RPO Suggests Reliable Revenue

Another metric that I find helpful when analyzing Autodesk is putting its remaining performance obligations [RPO] in relation to its realized revenue, either on a TTM basis or based on the current quarter times 4. RPO includes both billed and unbilled deferred revenue and essentially provides insight into how much revenue is already partly waiting in the balance sheet to be released going forward. I emphasize “partly” because unbilled deferred revenue is not recorded in the books under the accounting standards applied, although it is reported to investors.

With the current RPO/Revenue ratio above 100%, at least one year of the company’s current annual revenue run rate is already waiting to be realized. Add to that the fact that 97% of Autodesk’s revenue is considered recurring, most of which comes from subscriptions. This shows the company’s expectedly stable income stream.

Why Autodesk Should Be AI Resistant

Finally, this article focused on some financial technicalities and provided a big picture overview of where the company stands in terms of the stock market, while other analysts have already provided valuable and deep insight into Autodesk’s core business areas and their respective macro environments. At this point, let us just briefly go over key aspects.

Autodesk is one of the most internationally diversified US software companies, which is an aspect I personally highly appreciate. On a TTM basis, 44% of revenues came from the Americas, growing 16%; 38% came from EMEA, growing 18%; and 17% came from Asia Pacific, growing 9%.

Note that these growth rates are unadjusted ones and include beneficial short-term effects from transactional changes. Autodesk’s largest segment, AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations), contributes 49% of revenue and grows the fastest at 20% on a TTM basis. The rest, including CAD software, manufacturing simulation, and media animations, grew mid-single to low-double digits.

Given the fact that Autodesk tools are intertwined into lots of highly safety-critical projects like infrastructure or construction, I do not see it easily being replaced by AI. While AI is becoming better at a fast pace, I believe it still has a long way to go until it is accurate and “accountable” enough to shoulder critical tasks like that. This provides ample room for Autodesk to continue to position itself as a trusted partner who responsibly incorporates AI into its own specialized tools.

Takeaway

Autodesk runs a high-quality software business that I do not expect to be easily disrupted by AI due to the safety-critical nature of the projects it is used in. The company rather stands at the forefront of gradually letting AI flow into its products. Q3 earnings essentially confirmed Autodesk’s long-term path. At 30x adjusted P/E, the stock now trades at its own 3-year valuation average. Given some arguable ambiguity due to share-based compensation not considered in this adjusted metric, I stick to a Hold rating now that the stock has made up for its undervaluation in recent years, expecting risk-adequate returns going forward.

data.title: Atour Lifestyle: Bullish On Beat And Raise Quarter
data.description:
I've stayed positive on Atour Lifestyle Holdings (ATAT).

Q3 headline numbers were above consensus. It is expecting a substantial revenue surge for the full-year period.

The previous June 26, 2025 article drew attention to the promising signs for ATAT's retail and hospitality operations.

Recent Performance Was A Pleasant Surprise

The firm published a press release revealing its 3Q25 financials on Tuesday, Nov 25.

ATAT registered a normalized bottom line of CNY 0.49B in Jul-Sep '25, implying a +27% jump on YoY terms.

As per S&P Capital IQ data, its latest quarterly net profit exceeded the sell-side's projection by 3%.

Both of the company's divisions did well.

My late-June update highlighted that ATAT's "initial foray into bedding was focused on pillows, so there are opportunities in related products." I also cited management's remarks that "comforters as a proportion of total bedding revenue can possibly go up from one-fifth in 2024 to half in due course."

The group's 3Q2025 retail-related turnover was +76% higher YoY at CNY 0.85B.

According to its results slides, ATAT "launched upgraded products of the Deep Sleep Thermo-Regulating Comforter series" in the third quarter of the current year. This drove up the product line's "accumulated (unit) sales" to over 2M.

Its efforts to provide a wider range of merchandise for retail clients have paid off.

Separately, the YoY "Revenue Per Available Room/RevPAR" contraction for ATAT's hotels narrowed from 2Q25's -4.3% to 3Q25's -2.2%.

I touched on the improved Q3 RevPAR for Atour's peer H World (HTHT) in a Nov 18, 2025 write-up. My analysis indicated that HTHT benefited from "the booming leisure travel trend" and a slower pace of "supply growth" in China's hospitality market. These are also tailwinds for ATAT.

Internally, it worked hard at growing its footprint.

ATAT noted at the analyst briefing that "we opened 152 new hotels (in 3Q2025), a record high for a single quarter."

Its group-level top-line rose 38% to CNY 2.63B for 3Q25. This was equivalent to a 7% outperformance versus consensus (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Also, ATAT's EBITDA-to-sales expanded 120bps (basis points) from 24.9% for 2Q2025 to 26.1% in the recent quarter.

The enterprise's profitability was boosted by a favorable change in revenue composition and excellent cost control.

Its earnings announcement disclosed that "revenues from our leased hotels for the third quarter of 2025 decreased by 13.4%" because of "our product mix optimization." ATAT's "leased hotels" have lower margins than its "manachised (managed/franchised)" properties, due to the latter's asset-light nature.

Atour's "General & Administrative/G&A" expenses-to-revenue metric declined by -0.8ppts (percentage points) YoY and -0.2ppts QoQ to 3.4% for 3Q25.

The company's future is also bright, as I will elaborate in the next section.

Management Has Become More Optimistic About Full-Year Prospects

ATAT's FY25 topline growth guide was lifted from "+30%" earlier to "+35%" post-results.

In my Jun 26 piece, I mentioned "ATAT's bedding brand called Yaduo Planet, or YP was among the top three in terms of homecare category sales at China's mid-May to mid-June 618 Festival (Black Friday equivalent). I also predicted then that "another upward revision to its full-year retail sales outlook" was on the cards, considering "how well YP has done."

Indeed, the group decided to change the retail unit's sales increase guidance for '25 to +65%. The prior August and May forecasts were inferior at +60% and +50%, respectively.

YP still has a long runway, taking into account the whitespace.

Atour's Sleep-Focused Offerings

As per the chart presented above, ATAT's retail-related business is penetrating new sub-segments such as "loungewear" and "fitted sheet."

Also, its results call commentary pointed to the intention of "building a product portfolio that covers different scenarios" with "products like Deep Sleep Travel Pillow and Deep Sleep Pillow for Children." It's clear that YP is targeting younger consumers and travelers to broaden its customer base.

Appealing Shareholder Returns Are Supported By Generous Cash Distributions

It's encouraging to know that ATAT has a balanced capital allocation approach that isn't solely about investing for future growth.

Atour made a commitment at the 3Q25 earnings briefing that it will be "targeting a (dividends/buybacks) payout ratio of 100% (previously 50%) based upon the previous fiscal year's GAAP net income."

The 2025 consensus earnings estimate for ATAT is $223M based on S&P Capital IQ. Its current market cap is $5.2B.

That translates into an FY25 stockholder yield of 4.3% (223/5200).

You Would Contemplate These Risks

A weak Chinese economy could lead to a drop in demand for the company's retail- and hospitality-related products/services.

ATAT has pegged its capital return to a proportion of net income, rather than outlining an absolute figure. A lower-than-expected bottom-line will also likely translate into disappointments relating to repurchases and dividends.

My Conclusion

I continue to award a 'buy' rating to this name.

ATAT's EBITDA multiple has the potential to re-rate by +33% (16/12), using its listed comparable as a benchmark.

The consensus next twelve months' EV/EBITDAs for Atour and HTHT are 12x and 16x (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

A healthy growth outlook and an attractive shareholder yield justify richer valuations for ATAT.

data.title: Blackstone: A High Beta Stock Now At A Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This year’s third quarter of Blackstone Inc. (BX) was relatively weak compared to Q3 2024 primarily due to a sharp drop in Performance Allocations (carried interest perceived by Blackstone GPs in investment funds) due to headwinds in private equity and high stock market volatility. I believe nothing is fundamentally broken in the Blackstone model. Interest rate cuts (next one expected in December by the markets) and monetary easing will likely bring in massive capital injections and revive the alternative investment world.

The current dip in the stock price is an excellent entry price, as investments are likely to pick up again as capital is injected into the system. The change of Fed Chair in May, from Powell to Trump’s new pick (identity to be determined still), will also likely increase the probability of a Fed that goes in easing mode.

Business Overview

Blackstone is a leading asset management firm in the alternative space, and its revenue depends primarily on the AUM (from which fees are derived) it has, which grows as new products or funds are launched, but also based on the performance of their investments. The company has exposure to the entire alternative space, from private equity to infrastructure, from real estate to private debt and even to the nascent institutionalisation of cryptocurrencies. Quite naturally, the stock’s beta is significantly higher than 1, at around 1.75, as the company’s earnings are directly correlated to market liquidity, investor optimism and asset performance. With a presently rather restrictive monetary policy and relatively high-interest rates, the alternative sector, in particular private equity and real estate, have overall seen a slowdown in the dynamism and number of transactions since 2021. High treasury yields and strong stock market performance in the past years have also taken some liquidity from the alternatives. However, despite the slowdown, Blackstone has been able to consistently grow and, despite a somewhat mixed picture in the most recent earnings report, I believe the current dip is an ideal entry point.

Recent Earnings

Net income is flat on a January-September basis at around $4.1 billion in 2024 and 2025, give or take. However, on an LTM basis, net income is up +28%, which confirms the earnings growth picture. The issue is where the picture starts becoming less rosy: revenue in Q3 2025 is sharply down (-20%) relative to Q3 2024 despite relatively strong stock market performance. Quarterly revenue dropped from $3.7 billion to $3.1 billion, approximately.

Most of the revenue loss is in the line “Performance Allocations” which is roughly the carried interest perceived by GP interest and usually calculated based on their funds performances. This means Blackstone-led funds have underperformed significantly the S&P 500 in the same timeframe. I dove deeper in the financials supplement to understand if the Blackstone model is somehow broken: if it is, then the issue would be across all the segments where Blackstone has GPs.

The good news is that one segment alone has been a huge drag in the investment performance of Blackstone: real estate. The good news is the remaining segments have performed relatively well on a LTM basis and contributed to revenue and earnings with fees in rising AUM. Real estate has naturally seen its valuations drop (and arithmetically Blackstone’s real estate AUM) where mortgage rates in the US exceed 6% p.a. Interest rate cuts and capital injections should in theory lower real estate financing rates and bring some dynamism back to the segment in 2026. This could be a tremendous tailwind for Blackstone that, nonetheless, continues to show strong growth rates.

Valuation

With a revenue growth rate of 14.8% YoY and ROCE of 35.2%, Blackstone can be considered a growth stock. Its growth rates have consistently been in the double digits since years for both the top and bottom lines. And for a growth stock in the Financials segment, I believe the stock is now attractively valued after a 27% correction in its price in the last 12 months and -18% YTD.

The stock is now trading at a stark discount to its 5-year average. The stock is trading at a P/E of 26x based on full-year 2025 earnings (vs. 28x on its 5Y average) and a P/E of 22x on 2026 earnings, reflecting monetary and real estate tailwinds. The forward P/E is at a 20% discount to its 5Y average forward P/E and could lead to a strong rally leading into 2026. The stock’s beta of around 1.75 could lead the stock significantly higher as the stock market is rebounding now after last week’s dip, potentially fueled by a resuming of monetary easing. Comparing with peers such as BlackRock Inc. (BLK) that is trading at 19x 2026 earnings and KKR & Co Inc. (KKR) at 17x 2026 earnings, Blackstone does look expensive. But when you put the ROCE in perspective, Blackstone’s 35.2% far exceeds Blackrock’s 12.6% and KKR’s 8.9%, which in my opinion justifies the premium valuation.

Risk Factor To My Case

The key risk of this high beta stock is if inflation heats up again, causing a postponement of the Fed’s monetary easing and hence headwinds on asset valuations. This scenario is not probably at the moment despite recent scares due to a lack of data following the US government shutdown. Nonetheless, the stock can rapidly drop if the stock market needs to price high-interest rates for longer, again. The best way to mitigate this key risk is to incorporate the stock in a well-diversified investment portfolio oriented for the long term.

Bottom Line

Bottom line: Blackstone is a high beta stock with strong fundamentals and high-growth rates. The recent dip is an excellent entry point (or accumulation point, if, like me, you also already own the stock). Given the tailwinds I expect in 2026, I rate the stock a Strong Buy as long as the allocation is reasonable, within a sufficiently diversified portfolio to mitigate the inherent volatility of the stock.

data.title: Snowflake: The Stealthy Titan Ready For Explosive Growth In The AI Era
data.description:
The last several years have not been kind to Snowflake (NYSE: SNOW) investors, as the Information Technology ("IT") sector slowed in the immediate post-pandemic era. Between the beginning of 2022 and early 2025, the stock has been extraordinarily volatile and has yet to produce much Alpha for investors who bought it between 2020 and 2022.

The change from former Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Frank Slootman to Artificial Intelligence ("AI") savvy Sridhar Ramaswamy in February 2024 was supposed to cure some of its ills. Yet, it didn't immediately improve the company's fortunes. However, the CEO change has begun to pay off recently.

CEO Ramaswamy has steered the company toward greater product innovation and faster time-to-market through new AI features. CEO Ramaswamy said on the first quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 earnings call:

We have an incredible product momentum, and we are continuing to innovate at lightning speed. In fact, this quarter alone, we have brought over 125 product capabilities to market, a 100% increase over what we delivered in Q1 of last year.

The CEO's rapid innovation strategy helped increase first-quarter FY 2026 product revenue by 26% year over year, giving management the confidence to raise FY 2026 revenue guidance from $4.280 billion to $4.325 billion. Additionally, the CEO's focus on efficiency paid off; its first-quarter non-GAAP operating margin more than doubled year over year, from 4% to 9%. The prospect of higher revenue growth and increasing profitability was among the reasons the stock rose 13.43% the day after the company released first-quarter earnings.

The company delivered a repeat solid performance in the second quarter, achieving its first billion-dollar revenue quarter. It grew product revenue by 32% year over year in the second quarter of FY 2026, and non-GAAP operating margin expanded from 5% to 11% over the previous year's second quarter. Management raised FY 2026 revenue guidance again in the second quarter to $4.395 billion and FY 2026 non-GAAP operating margin to 9%. Investors responded by bidding the stock up by 20.27% to $241.00 the day after the company reported second-quarter earnings on August 28.

Pay close attention to the company's upcoming third-quarter earnings release on December 3, 2025, for indications that management will raise FY 2026 revenue for the third time this year and non-GAAP operating margin guidance for the second time this year. In that case, analysts will likely respond by raising earnings per share ("EPS") estimates, resulting in lower forward-looking valuations and making the stock look more attractive to investors.

Despite the recent perception that the stock is doing well, some are reluctant to invest in it. The market worries about decelerating revenue growth. Since the beginning of 2022, the company's revenue growth has slowed from over triple digits to its current 31.78%. Although its revenue growth may have stabilized over the last several quarters, some still worry that it will deteriorate further in an intensely competitive market.

Some rapidly growing companies, like Snowflake, choose to use non-GAAP metrics to highlight their core profitability. Still, some investors object to non-GAAP metrics because they can sometimes exclude important non-cash costs that can affect the company's performance. For instance, Snowflake's second quarter FY 2026 non-GAAP results excluded $389.264 million in stock-based compensation, which is 30.19% of revenue, a relatively high figure. The company's substantial SBC may deter some investors, as it could dilute existing investors' holdings.

Additionally, some dislike the company's GAAP unprofitability. Although its GAAP profitability has been improving over the last few years, its margins haven't been expanding fast enough for some. Snowflake's third quarter FY 2026 trailing twelve 12-month ("TTM") GAAP operating margin is still only -34.71%. If you focus on GAAP metrics, you may be deterred by its current valuation, given its lack of profitability and intense competition in the data market.

Lastly, the company is still recovering from a 2024 security incident in which a bad actor stole information from Snowflake customers. In 2025, the Cloud Security Alliance published a blog post discussing the breach.

Although the breach didn't hurt Snowflake financially, it may have damaged its reputation in 2024, leading to a drop in the stock price. It took until near the beginning of 2025 to put the incident behind it and for the stock to recover.

So, is Snowflake a good investment moving forward? My answer is yes because the company brought Ramaswamy in as CEO to capitalize on the massive shift towards AI and Machine Learning (ML). As quality data is the fuel that powers AI, Snowflake is sitting in the perfect position. When Frank Slootman was CEO, Snowflake primarily provided data warehousing services to improve customer efficiency and enable traditional analytics. Some thought the company had a limited Total Addressable Market (TAM) in the cloud, as a data warehousing company.

These days are different. AI became one of the most significant trends in IT after 2023. As AI has proliferated, the importance of high-quality data has grown because the results AI models produce are only as good as the data a company uses to train them. As the company expands its product line to accommodate AI, its TAM is growing far beyond what some initially thought.

CEO Ramaswamy discussed at the Goldman Sachs Communicopia + Technology Conference 2025 how businesses are only now realizing they need high-quality data to effectively use AI to improve their operations.

The company is now actively moving beyond its pre-AI role as a simple analytics platform to a data lifecycle manager focused on AI. Without diving too far into the weeds of data management, adopting a strategy to address the entire data lifecycle significantly expands Snowflake's TAM. Now, it competes in areas traditionally dominated by other vendors.

If you believe AI will continue to proliferate and that AI infrastructure companies will outperform the market over the next five to ten years, then consider an investment in Snowflake.

Snowflake Intelligence

This company and its products are among the more difficult topics for me to write about because it's hard to explain how Snowflake enables AI through its data platform in a way that is understandable to people with little background in data management or AI, while also being factually accurate. I had to rewrite this section several times to make it understandable to those without data management expertise.

Before discussing Snowflake Intelligence, let's first discuss Snowflake Cortex. It's essential to understand how all of these products relate to each other, or else one can easily get confused when management mentions them during conference calls.

Traditionally, enterprises would transfer data to a third-party AI engine for processing. However, Snowflake Cortex flips that process: it is an AI engine that travels to where the data is in Snowflake's cloud and processes it. The genius of this system is that data security theoretically improves because data never leaves its secure storage. Cortex manages Large Language Models (LLMs) from several popular providers, including OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic, Mistral, Meta Platforms (META), and Arctic, Snowflake's proprietary LLM model. Professional technical teams use SQL to query data in Cortex, or use Python to build custom applications that integrate with Cortex or support advanced data science tasks.

To make the power of Cortex available to users who don't understand SQL, the company created several applications that run on top of Cortex, acting as interpreters that convert natural language questions into SQL queries that Cortex understands. These applications include Snowflake Copilot, an AI assistant for developers (Data Analysts, Data Engineers). Although Data Analysts and Data Engineers often understand SQL, it is sometimes more convenient to pose questions to the data in plain English rather than in a programming language. Another application is Cortex Search, which allows applications or business users to search unstructured data (videos, images, social media posts, and emails) using plain English. Cortex Analyst allows business users to search structured data (e.g., an Excel sheet) using plain English. Snowflake Cortex Intelligence is an application that allows non-technical executives to access all the company's data, both structured and unstructured, in plain English. Cortex Analyst and Cortex Search are simply a part of Cortex Intelligence.

Business users often use Cortex Intelligence as part of a unified experience to simultaneously search structured and unstructured data. However, developers might configure and use Cortex Analyst and Cortex Search separately as distinct tools within agentic workflows or applications.

So, how does Snowflake Intelligence help the company improve revenue growth?

The company uses a consumption-based model. Anything that encourages companies to use Snowflake more increases the company's revenue. Snowflake Intelligence encourages substantially greater data use by enabling more non-technical business people to access data, not just data analysts who write SQL, which generates significantly more revenue for the company.

Snowflake Cortex Intelligence is central to the CEO's strategy to make Snowflake the "all-encompassing data platform" for AI. The company is making significant progress on that strategy. CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy said on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

Today, AI is a core reason why customers are choosing Snowflake, influencing nearly 50% of new logos won in Q2. And once they are on our platform, AI becomes a cornerstone of their strategy, powering 25% of all deployed use cases with over 6,100 accounts using Snowflake's AI every week. We have embedded AI across the data life cycle to accelerate analytics, transform workflows and even power migrations.

In a video titled "Snowflake CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy on Using Data to Create Simple, Reliable AI for Businesses" by Sequoia Capital, CEO Ramaswamy stated, "Even with GPT-4 right out of the box, you end up getting 45% reliability, meaning that it gets half the questions wrong that it tries to answer." He later added that right now Snowflake has its accuracy "well into the 90s" and "racing to get to 99% reliability." If it accomplishes that, it would likely give the company a robust competitive advantage over many AI solutions on the market, especially given Snowflake's reputation for data governance and security.

Last, but not least, the company's business model of embedding AI tools directly into the platform where the data already resides creates a massive switching-cost moat, making it more challenging for customers to move their data to a competing vendor for AI modeling and inference services. As a result of its move into AI, Snowflake should be able to maintain its world-class net retention rate.

Snowflake's net revenue retention rate ("NRR") of 125% means that its existing customers from the previous 12 months have increased their spending by 25% from a year earlier, when including downgrades and cancellations. Investors typically consider an NRR above 125% to be top-tier Software-as-a-Service ("SaaS") and often award a premium valuation for maintaining an NRR around 125%. When we get to the valuation section, and you consider some of Snowflake's seemingly astronomical valuation metrics, remember its NRR number because it's among several reasons the market allows Snowflake to trade at a premium above where most value investors believe it should trade. Suppose Snowflake's AI initiatives help it maintain an NRR above 125%, above 20% revenue growth, and progress toward GAAP profitability over the next five years. In that case, the stock will likely continue to rise despite the perceived high valuation.

Valuation

Snowflake's TTM price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 20.57, which is above its three-year median but below its five-year median, which some might interpret as a sign of fair valuation. Suppose it traded at its three-year median; its stock price would be $231.94, a 9% decline below its November 19 closing price of $252.98. If the stock traded at its five-year median, its price would be $296.02, a 17% rise from its November 19 closing price.

The following table compares Snowflake's two-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) with several cloud players. Very few publicly traded companies offer the exact services that Snowflake does as a pure play. Therefore, I used some high-growth SaaS companies that are not precisely in the same business.

Company Name Two-year forward P/S ratio Two-year forward estimated revenue growth Two-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin

Gross Margin

TTM

Snowflake 15.00 23.90% 0.63 17.83% 65.45% -34.71% -26.72%
Palantir (PLTR) 66.15 40.03% 1.65 46.05% 80.81% 10.83% 18.88%
Cloudflare (NET) 25.93 27.39% 0.95 10.27% 75.19% -9.35% -2.96
Confluent (CFLT) 5.86 16.54% 0.35 3.51% 74.09% -34.13% -14.28%

Generally, when investors compare relative P/S/G ratios, the market considers the company with the highest ratio as overvalued relative to those with lower ratios.

The most important profitability metric for a SaaS company is gross margin, because it is the best measure of the quality of the revenue stream. So, we will compare Snowflake's two-year forward P/S, estimated revenue growth, and two-year P/S/G Ratio and median gross margin. I exclude Snowflake from the median calculation below, so it won't affect the benchmark I compare it against. The company with the most similar business model to Snowflake in the above group is MongoDB. The company with the most similar growth stage and market growth expectation is Datadog.

Company Two-year Forward P/S Estimated Revenue Growth Two-year P/S/G Ratio

Gross Margin
Snowflake 15.00 23.90% 0.63 65.45%
Datadog 15.45 20.70% 0.75 71.99%
MongoDB 10.02 16.91% 0.59 79.91%
Median (Excluding Snowflake) 15.45 20.70% 0.75 75.19%

Since Snowflake has a lower two-year P/S/G ratio than the median in the comparison and a lower P/S/G than Datadog and MongoDB, the market may consider it undervalued based solely on P/S/G alone. However, some investors may penalize Snowflake for its lower gross margin relative to the median. As a result, depending on how much you weigh gross margin into the valuation, you may consider it anywhere from fairly valued to slightly undervalued.

The company's TTM non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 231.92, well above the IT sector median of 22.68, indicating potential overvaluation.

Snowflake's forward 2026 P/E to analysts' estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 4.92, calculated by dividing its FY 2026 forward P/E of 212.61 by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 43.25%. Generally, growth investors consider a PEG ratio above 2.0 as a sign of an overvalued stock. Suppose the stock traded at a FY 2026 PEG ratio of 2.0; its price would be 102.94, a 59% decline from its November 19 closing price of $252.98.

However, be careful not to take this calculated price target based on a PEG valuation rule of 2.0 too literally, because if analysts raise their expected future earnings growth rate significantly faster than the stock price rises, the valuation can decrease while the stock price increases.

When Seeking Alpha published my July 9, 2025, article on Snowflake, the stock price was $224.6, and its FY 2026 forward PEG ratio was 6.03. At the time I write this article on November 19, the stock price closed the day at $ 252.98, up 12.63% from my July article. Yet, the FY 2026 PEG ratio shrank by 18.40% to 4.92. In other words, the stock price rose since July as its PEG valuation improved, driven by analysts raising their EPS estimates from $1.11 in my July article to $1.19 as of November 19. That's why you need to be careful in believing strict valuation rules too strongly. The market may "overvalue" Snowflake based on various measures and traditional rules. However, if the company's growth continues to exceed expectations, the stock may still rise, despite being "overvalued."

Its price-to-FCF of 116.06 is well above its three-year median of 86.64, a potential sign of overvaluation. Suppose the stock sold at its three-year median; the stock price would be $189.99, a 25.34% decline below its November 18 closing price.

The following reverse discounted cash flow uses a terminal growth rate of 3%, reflecting what I believe will be the U.S. market's long-term inflation rate. This reverse DCF uses a 14% discount rate, based on Snowflake's weighted average cost of capital ("WACC") of 14.03%, reflecting a high risk level. Given its ROIC of -26.72%, the company's returns don't justify its cost of capital, suggesting Snowflake destroys capital as it grows. Some investors may avoid the stock for that reason alone.

This reverse DCF analysis utilizes the following TTM FCF data from YCharts.

Reverse DCF

The fourth quarter of fiscal year (FY) 2025 reported TTM FCF

 $734 million
Terminal growth rate 3%
Discount Rate 14%
Years 1-10 growth rate 41%
Stock Price (November 18, 2025, closing price) $254.51
Terminal FCF value $23.481 billion
Discounted Terminal Value $57.582 billion
TTM FCF margin 17.83%

According to this reverse DCF, Snowflake needs to grow its FCF by 41% annually over the next 10 years to justify its November 18 closing price, which will be highly challenging in this very competitive environment. Therefore, the market likely overvalues the stock based on this reverse DCF.

No matter how you look at it, this stock trades at a premium valuation. Seeking Alpha's quant rates Snowflake an F. A premium valuation doesn't necessarily mean the price won't keep rising. Still, at this valuation, investors should go into it with eyes wide open, recognizing that this stock has high valuation risk.

Risks

This stock has the potential for significant volatility. The company uses a consumption model, charging customers based on actual usage rather than a fixed fee. This model works well when the economy has a tailwind. However, when the economy slows and customers spend less, revenue could decline rapidly. At the stock's current premium, the market is unlikely to react well to a surprise revenue decline.

Additionally, the company is increasingly hitching its wagon to AI growth. Although the proliferation of AI is a solid growth engine that should support the company's steady double-digit revenue growth, if AI enterprise adoption runs out of steam, its revenue growth could spiral downward rapidly. The stock price would likely deteriorate at a similar rate.

Snowflake faces significant execution risk at its current scale and valuation. To sustain revenue growth, management has accelerated hiring in sales and marketing (S&M). Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Michael P. Scarpelli said on the second-quarter FY 2026 earnings call, "I would say we've actually hired more sales and marketing people in the first 6 months of this year on a net basis than we did in the prior 2 years combined." If the company's revenue underperforms after its enormous investment in sales, profitability could worsen, and the market may punish the stock.

It won't be easy for Snowflake to maintain its current revenue growth rate in the highly competitive data management and AI market. Especially since some believe that some competitors have better solutions. A few of Snowflake's prominent competitors include Databricks, Amazon (AMZN) Redshift, Alphabet's (GOOGL)(GOOG) Google BigQuery, Microsoft (MSFT) Azure Synapse, Oracle (ORCL), Palantir, and Cloudera. The sheer number of high-end competitors highlights that this market is highly contested. Although Snowflake was a first mover in the cloud data warehousing market, it is now expanding into new markets while maintaining superior execution to retain its leading position in data and gain share in the AI infrastructure market. There is no guarantee it can accomplish that goal. If competition slows Snowflake's revenue growth rate or squeezes its margins, the market will likely sell off the stock.

Snowflake remains a buy

This stock is not for everyone. Clint Eastwood once said in the movie Magnum Force in 1973, "A man's gotta know his limitations." If you invest in this stock, make sure that you understand your limitations first. The stock is high risk, has an elevated valuation, and not everyone will have the courage to hold this one if things go wrong. When this stock does sell off, it often happens quickly, giving investors little time to push the eject button. So, go in with eyes wide open to the risks before plunking down your hard-earned money. Value investors and risk-averse investors may be better off avoiding the stock.

However, the high potential upside might entice risk-tolerant investors. Snowflake's TTM revenue is $4.116 billion, and analysts' estimated FY 2026 (Approximately calendar Year 2025) revenue is $4.61 billion, which is 2.34% of its estimated TAM of $196.98 billion (calculated using a CAGR calculator and reverse CAGR calculator on data from Snowflake's TAM chart on page 12 of the second quarter FY 2026 investor presentation).

Analysts estimate that revenue will grow at a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 22.53% from $4.61 billion in FY 2026 to $ 10.39 billion in FY 2030 (approximately calendar year 2029), representing 2.92% of the company's estimated FY 2030 (calendar year 2029) TAM of $355 billion. If Snowflake's estimated TAM and analysts' annual revenue growth rates are correct, even with a 22.53% revenue CAGR over the next four years, its market share will still be less than 3%, leaving plenty of revenue growth runway through FY 2035.

Snowflake's expanding non-GAAP operating margin, which I highlighted earlier in the article, indicates the company has operating leverage and should grow non-GAAP operating profit faster than revenue. If the company lowers its SBC percentage of revenue (revenue growth outpaces SBC grant growth), it can make significant progress toward GAAP profitability, which the market would well receive.

If you are an aggressive growth investor with high risk tolerance, Snowflake may be an excellent investment over the next decade. I recommend a buy for aggressive growth investors.

data.title: Investment Grade Outlook: Insights For November
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head of North America Investment Grade Credit and Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

October was another positive month for fixed income. The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index rose about 38 basis points (bps), which put it up 7.29% year to date.1 The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index rose 62 bps, up 6.80% year to date.2 But the Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cut led to a selloff. What does it mean for investment-grade bonds and the economy? We answer this and more in our Q&A on how we’re navigating the bond market in November.

Craig: October was a positive month, but it might have been an even better month if bonds hadn’t sold off by roughly 75 bps-1% after the Fed cut rates at the end of the month. What was driving the roughly 1.5% total returns before the Fed meeting?

Matt: Credit spreads were basically unchanged month-to-date in October, up until the Fed meeting. Spreads have already been tight, but that has been justified by solid fundamentals and good liquidity - both of which remain intact. Rates were already lower by about 12-20 bps across the yield curve, driven by expectations of multiple Fed cuts and some concern about softer economic momentum. But the real driver of total return continues to be positive carry. With starting yields in the 4.5-5% range, your baseline return from carry is about 40 bps per month.

Craig: The Fed cut rates by 25 bps, which was exactly what the market was pricing. Yet, rates and stocks sold off and spreads widened. So, clearly, investors didn’t love what they heard. What do you think spooked markets?

Matt: The Fed-issued Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) statement noted that inflation had moved up since earlier in the year and remained elevated. It emphasized uncertainty around the economic outlook and downside risks to employment. But the key line was Chairman Jerome Powell saying December is not a foregone conclusion for another cut. So, even though we got a cut - and quantitative tightening (QT) ends in December - the meeting was still interpreted as hawkish. Markets are now pricing fewer cuts next year.

Craig: There’s significant disagreement among Fed officials on the path of rates and the economy. We’ve also heard mirror views from financial professionals and clients. One financial professional called this the “owl market” because everyone’s looking in all directions, waiting for something to go wrong. But nothing has yet. Can you explain your views and how you’re positioned?

Matt: We think the economy is still fundamentally solid. Consumers have slowed a bit but remain healthy, corporate balance sheets look good, and we’re not seeing the kinds of excesses you’d expect before a downturn. So our base case isn’t a recession, it’s a moderation. But even in that environment, policy rates don’t need to stay this restrictive. We think the Fed is already effectively in restrictive territory. As inflation gradually drifts lower, it has room to bring the policy rate down toward something closer to neutral, which is around 3%. So, we expect a world where growth is OK but rates migrate lower. That means we’re running a bit longer with bonds at the front end of duration and the yield curve than we had earlier in the year, and continue to lean into high-quality spread sectors where fundamentals remain strong.

Craig: Whether we get a slowdown or the economy continues to do well, we believe both scenarios are positive for bonds. Investors must agree based on the significant flows into the asset class.3 But much of that has been going into passive, even though active managers historically have regularly added value in fixed income.4

Emily: More than 20 years of rolling three-year periods of data shows that at pretty much any fee load, there has been an advantage to being in active fixed income for a core plus mandate.5 A core plus fund holds a core of investment grade securities and adds a layer of higher-yielding bonds to potentially increase returns. Plus, with the upcoming 2026 market duration mandate, a US Treasury clearing regulation requiring a large portion of US Treasury cash and repurchase agreement (repo) market transactions to be cleared through an SEC-approved central clearing agency, it’s potentially challenging for long-term investors to make a case for fixed income outside of active.

Footnotes

1 Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Oct. 31, 2025. The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes US dollar-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers.

2 Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Oct. 31, 2025. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment grade, fixed-rate bond market.

3 Source: Morningstar, $116,970 million estimated net flows into the Taxable Bond category as of Oct. 31, 2025.

4 Source: Active vs. Passive Report, Callan Institute, Third Quarter 2025.

5 Source: Active vs. Passive Report, Callan Institute, Third Quarter 2025. Over 3-year rolling periods dating the last 20 years, the median active manager in the Core Plus category outperforms the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index at least 74% of time across all fee hurdles. The average annualized excess return (gross) of the median manager is 0.97%.

Important information

NA5005285

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

A basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years.

Fixed income investments are subject to the credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is a committee of the Federal Reserve Board that meets regularly to set monetary policy, including the interest rates that are charged to banks.

Hawkish describes a central bank or policymaker's preference for a tighter monetary policy, typically to combat inflation.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

A policy rate is the rate used by central banks to implement or signal their monetary policy stance.

Positive carry is an investing strategy that uses leverage to increase your returns.

Carry is the expected return from holding a bond, assuming its price remains stable. Bond income/yields are higher than the cost of financing.

Quantitative tightening (QT) is a monetary policy used by central banks to normalize balance sheets.

Spread represents the difference between two values or asset returns.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic, and political conditions.

Tightening monetary policy includes actions by a central bank to curb inflation.

The yield curve plots interest rates at a set point of time for bonds of equal credit quality but differing maturity dates in order to project future interest rate changes and economic activity.

data.title: HYI Seems Less Competitive In The Market Today
data.description:
When we talk about Western Asset High Yield Opportunity Fund Inc (HYI), what comes to mind is a CEF with a competitive yield in the high yield bond market. And indeed it seemed exactly like a competitive solution on the market for those who want a closed-end solution to optimize distributions. What I appreciate little at this moment, however, is the market’s reaction to the recent tender offer. But even more, the macroeconomic evidence related to the high yield market, especially in the deep junk segment. But let’s look more closely at what this means and how this impacts a CEF like HYI.

To know

HYI is a closed-end fund (CEF) high yield no leverage managed by Western Asset (Franklin Templeton). The fund is created with a primary objective of generating high income, and then with a secondary one of maintaining a capital appreciation. It does this by following a defined structure: minimum investment 80% in corporate bond high yield (junk bonds) and maintaining the possible possibility of using derivatives (CDS, forwards, futures) up to 20% of NAV for risk management.

Participation in the fund involves incurring an expense ratio of 0.90%; a not insignificant cost however if we consider the income bond nature of the fund.

The history

The fund was supposed to terminate (liquidate) on September 30, 2025. In June 2025 shareholders voted to convert it into a perpetual fund, eliminating the liquidation date. The new name is Western Asset High Yield Opportunity Fund Inc. (still HYI). This, if on one hand is reassuring, on the other hand transforms HYI into a fund at risk of persistent discount in my opinion; the fact is that the transition to perpetual after a tender offer of 43% always signals in my opinion a clear division in the investor base. Not surprisingly, the assets are today at about $155M, which in my view cannot be considered high for a HY CEF. To confirm this, the fact that today it has a discount between Avg. Market Price vs NAV at Close of -6.71%. But naturally, the question we all ask ourselves is: is it a moment, or better to move to alternative solutions?

The holding distribution

Moving to the holding distribution, today we note about 270 holdings, where no position exceeds 3.3%, which indicates a good distribution. With about 73% towards US securities and the rest distributed towards smaller markets.

With a sector distribution of this type:

  • Consumer cyclical: 21.68%
  • Communications: 19.72%
  • Energy: 13.01%
  • Consumer non-cyclical: 9.33%
  • Finance: 8.20%

It is immediately noticeable that Consumer cyclical + Communications today represents over 40% of the overall portfolio. Being very cyclical segments, they typically also have the highest historical default rate in the HY category.

This gives the fund a rating distribution skewed towards B–CCC, as follows:

  • BBB: 5.29%
  • BB: 24.84%
  • B: 34.98%
  • CCC: 23.91%
  • CC, C, D: ~4%
  • NR: 4.76%

It seems clear to me that most of the distribution leans towards B/BB and CCC segments, which denotes a certain risk propensity not to be treated with indifference.

Distribution

Moving to the first objective of the fund: the distribution. We are talking about a Yield at market price of 9.74%. The distribution is monthly, and it seems that about $0.16 ROC out of $1.14 of total distribution.

Here, the spread between NAV yield (9.56%) and price yield (10.25%) reflects the -6.7% discount of the price.

The statistical data

Let’s start from the fact that the Alpha (%) against the Bloomberg HY B-index was negative. While the Beta remained close to 1, with a moderate tracking error; elements that indicate that HYI does not deviate too much from the benchmark. The information ratio instead at 3 years is -0.61 which signals a risk-adjusted performance that is negative, which also somewhat corresponds to the lack of alpha generation. The problem is not so much in the upside capture, which is typically over 90, but it has a higher downside capture ratio (not so good). At 3 years: 140.66.

The peers

I think it may be interesting to compare HYI to an ETF like iShares BB Rated Corporate Bond ETF (HYBB) oriented toward more quality high yield and VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (ANGL).

But expanding the time horizon, even to 5 years, the problem reappears, even looking at total return. In fact, the outperformance did not arrive, neither compared to HYBB nor to ANGL, even before the tender offer, despite the CCC component having outperformed in the post-2022 recovery.

Risks and context

The momentum of the entire HY sector is slowing down. The reason? clearly an uncertain monetary context: inflation exceeds 3%, and this evidence slows the Fed in the rate-cutting process. At the same time, however, negative prospects are intensifying, and in my opinion this is clear when looking at the divergence that has emerged between the LEI and CEI indices. And economic turbulence/monetary uncertainty does not typically play in favor of HY. Not so much because of their sensitivity to rates, since they have minimal durations; but rather because of their sensitivity to credit risk.

The OAS today is at minimal levels, and seems to have reversed course.

An element that could signal a reversal of default rate expectations. Therefore, net of the price problem after the tender offer, personally I also see a concrete risk of underperformance in the HY segment, and especially the deep junk tiers, such as CCC, which in any case also make up HYI.

Conclusion

Personally, considering the two risks of the moment: the structural one of HYI, and the contextual one of the High Yield segment, I remain not very inclined to build positions now, but also to maintain them. Sure, the CEF could still have been a competitive solution, interesting for an exposure to segments less covered by ETFs such as the more deep junk ones, but today I see greater risks, which personally, and I emphasize personally, I prefer not to face.

data.title: Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Technology stocks—and artificial intelligence (AI)-related stocks in particular—continued to dominate headlines throughout the third quarter, providing a strong tailwind for the large-cap growth segment, which advanced 10.51%, as measured by the Russell 1000® Growth Index. Midway through August, small-cap stocks staged a notable surge with the Russell 2000® Index advancing 12.39%, its strongest quarterly performance since 2021. Small-cap performance was supported by growing expectations that the Federal Reserve would cut short-term interest rates—a move that ultimately materialized later in the quarter—alongside optimism that tariffs might prove less detrimental to smaller companies than initially feared. With the overall market moving higher and with a general “risk-on” sentiment, higher beta (more volatile), lower quality, and non-earning stocks performed much better in the quarter than their higher quality counterparts.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Growth Fund returned -1.95% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell 2500 TM Growth Index, which returned 10.73%. Stock selection in the industrials and information technology sectors detracted from performance, while stock selection in the consumer staples sector and an underweight position in the consumer discretionary sector contributed positively to performance.

Celsius and AAON were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Energy drink company Celsius (CELH) continued its upward trajectory, benefiting from robust demand for sugar-free functional beverages. Distribution gains in international markets and strong sell-through in U.S. retail channels contributed to top-line momentum. A recent acquisition has expanded the company’s product portfolio and enhanced its competitive positioning. Brand strength and marketing execution remain key drivers of share gains in the energy category.
  • AAON (AAON) manufactures heating, air conditioning, and ventilation (HVAC) equipment for commercial and industrial use. The company’s stock performed well after enterprise resource planning (ERP) software implementation challenges related to strong rooftop bookings subsided. The company also benefited from robust cooling demand from data centers.

FactSet Research Systems and Ryan Specialty were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • FactSet (FDS), a provider of financial data, market analysis, and insights, faced margin pressure stemming from elevated investment in AI and data infrastructure, while organic growth remained muted due to end-market headwinds facing asset managers.
  • Ryan Specialty (RYAN) is a leading international specialty insurance firm. After an extended period of sharply rising rates, commercial property insurance premiums have been declining, which has caused the company’s organic growth pace to decelerate.
